topics.title: Tease rainbow snake surprise in README coming soon section
topics.decisions: The change was placed in "Coming soon" rather than the "Features" section because the rainbow snake feature has not yet been implemented — the branch at commit time was identical to main with no feature code. Listing it as a shipped feature would have been inaccurate, so the tease was scoped to the roadmap section instead.

entries.recap:
The snake game now has a full automated test suite that runs on every code change. Eight end-to-end tests cover the main menu, single-player game startup, pause and reset behavior, death and game-over flow, and Iron Snake mode — all driven through keyboard input and verified against visible on-screen text and screen transitions. A mid-run fix was needed for the Iron Snake goal readout, which displays as a formatted string rather than a bare number.

The suite runs automatically in GitHub Actions on every push and pull request to the main branch at no cost, with the full test report saved as a downloadable artifact for seven days. Developers can also run the suite locally with a single command, or open an interactive visual runner to step through tests in a real browser.

topics.title: Playwright end-to-end test harness added to snake game
topics.decisions:
- **Chromium only to start**: The harness targets a single browser rather than the full Playwright cross-browser matrix. Adding Firefox and WebKit later is straightforward, but starting lean keeps CI fast and avoids flakiness from browser-specific quirks before the suite is proven stable.
- **Keyboard-and-DOM testing strategy**: The game renders entirely on a canvas with no state exposed to the page, so tests cannot inspect game internals directly. The approach drives the game through keyboard input and asserts on observable DOM signals — screen visibility toggled by a CSS class, and text readouts for score, level, and goal — rather than canvas pixels. This keeps tests resilient to visual changes while still covering real user flows.
- **Deterministic death over score-based assertions**: The game-over test steers the snake into the right wall from its fixed starting position rather than waiting for a score increment or fruit collection. Wall collision is guaranteed within a bounded number of game ticks, making the test reliable without any random-seed control or internal game hooks. A `window.__game` handle for deeper determinism was explicitly deferred as a follow-up.

entries.recap:
Iron Snake boards now contain interior holes — dark voids surrounded by the playable surface that the snake must navigate around. Colliding with a hole is immediately fatal, food never spawns inside one, and the bot correctly routes around them. The holes grow in number and size as the player advances through levels: early boards have one or two small single-cell gaps, while later boards host several larger cavities, giving the mode a natural difficulty ramp without any manual tuning.

On level transitions, holes are carved after the new board shape is finalized, and the area immediately around the snake is always protected, so a hole can never appear under the snake or directly in its path the moment play resumes. The death screen now distinguishes between hitting the outer wall and falling into an interior hole, in both single-player and two-player modes.

topics.title: Add interior obstacle holes to Iron Snake boards
topics.decisions:
- **Reusing existing invariant checkers as hole validators**: Rather than writing new safety logic, the hole-carving pass reuses the same connectivity check and minimum-width morphological open that already guard the base shape. This means any hole that survives is automatically guaranteed to be a genuine interior gap with adequate clearance — the minimum-width check rejects holes placed within one cell of another wall, so thin corridors and edge bites are impossible without any extra code.
- **Anchoring placement attempts on known playable cells**: Random placement across the full bounding grid wastes most of the attempt budget on off-board positions, since the playable blob covers only about 57% of the grid. Anchoring each attempt on an actual on-board cell and requiring the entire footprint to be surrounded by playable neighbors (the "interior only" constraint) was the change that made hole placement reliable on small early boards, where deep-interior positions are a minority.
- **Level-scaled count and size rather than fixed tuning constants**: Tying hole count and maximum footprint to the current level means the difficulty increase is automatic as the arena grows across levels, and no separate difficulty curve needs to be maintained. The play-test feedback during the session (holes should be randomly larger and more numerous at later levels) was the direct prompt for this approach over the original area-only scaling.

entries.recap:
Iron Snake mode now animates the board transition between levels: the playable region reshapes cell-by-cell from the completed level's outline into the next level's shape, with each beat of the reshape held for roughly the same duration as one snake move. The arena grows outward from the center in a wave, and old-only cells peel away as the new boundary fills in. The snake and food remain visible and grounded throughout, and normal play resumes automatically once the reshape completes.

Two bugs discovered during testing were also fixed. The snake was teleporting to the center of the new board on every level advance — the new approach generates the board around the snake's current position rather than relocating the snake onto a freshly generated board, so the snake never moves. A separate issue caused some level transitions to show no shape change at all: the board generation was seeding from the previous board's shape, which was already at target fill when the grid size stayed the same. Seeding from the snake's bounding box instead ensures a genuinely different shape is generated every level regardless of whether the grid grows.

topics.title: Board grows fresh random shape around stationary snake each level
topics.decisions:
- **Seed from the snake, not the old board**: The previous grow approach embedded the old board into the new grid and added rooms until target fill. When the grid size stayed the same (common at early levels floored at size 12), the old board was already at target fill and zero new rooms were added — producing an identical shape. Seeding from only the snake's bounding box means the surrounding region is always regenerated fresh, so the shape changes every level regardless of whether the grid grows.
- **Grow around the snake rather than relocate it**: The original design generated a random board first and then relocated the snake to a preferred anchor, which caused the visible teleport the user reported. Growing the board around the snake's current cells eliminates relocation entirely — the snake never moves, and the board is guaranteed to include every cell the snake occupies by construction. This also simplified the code substantially by removing the multi-tier rigid-placement and Warnsdorff fallback chain.

topics.title: Snake stays in place during level transitions instead of teleporting
topics.decisions: The relocation approach was originally designed to place the snake at a "preferred anchor" (board center) on the freshly generated board, which required capturing the snake's shape as head-relative offsets before the board was replaced and then re-seating it afterward. Keeping the snake stationary made this entire mechanism unnecessary: the board is now generated to fit the snake rather than the snake being fitted to the board. This removed a significant amount of placement complexity and eliminated the teleport as a side effect.

topics.title: Level transitions animate board reshape cell-by-cell at snake-move pace
topics.decisions:
- **Discrete steps held at move cadence rather than a continuous real-time tween**: An initial implementation ran the morph over a fixed 600ms wall-clock duration, which the user could not see because the reduced-motion gate bypassed it entirely. Re-pacing to the snake's own move interval makes the transition feel deliberate and game-native — each reshape beat corresponds to one tick the snake would have taken — and remains visible at any game speed.
- **Mutate the live board mask each frame rather than compositing snapshots**: All three board-drawing functions read a single mask field, so feeding the renderer an interpolated display mask each frame causes the floor tiles, grid lines, and border outline to all reshape for free with no changes to any rendering code. A snapshot crossfade approach would have required offscreen canvas compositing and would not have produced the cell-by-cell geometric effect the user described.
- **Inside-out wave ordering for the changed cells**: Cells that differ between the old and new shape are revealed in order of distance from the board center, so the arena appears to grow outward and old-only cells peel away as the wave passes. This was chosen over a random or scanline order because it reads as a coherent spatial event rather than noise, and it required only a sort — no additional data structures.

entries.recap:
The landing page preview was substantially upgraded to match the real game's visual quality. The snake now renders with a taller head block featuring eyes that face the direction of travel, and a tapered tail that narrows to a point. Each page load randomly commits to either an Iron Snake carved board or a classic rectangle, with a fresh shape generated on every life. A small overlay in the corner of the preview names the bot currently playing, and the bot changes on every reset — so the preview now showcases the variety of strategies available in the full game.

A long-standing bug in the bot movement system was also fixed. When a bot detected it was stuck in a loop, it would switch to an escape strategy and never return to its own algorithm. The escape phase now runs for a fixed window of time, after which the bot resumes its normal decision-making with a clean slate. Bots will still break out of loops when needed, but they no longer get permanently locked into the escape behavior.

topics.title: Landing page preview upgraded with real snake graphics, random bots, and Iron Snake boards
topics.decisions:
- **Per-page-load vs. per-life board type**: Iron vs. classic is decided once when the page loads rather than re-randomized on every death. During verification, screenshots taken at random moments almost always landed on the classic board at a 50/50 per-life split, because the snake survives much longer on the roomy rectangle. Locking the style per visit means a given page load consistently showcases one style across all lives, and visitors who reload will see the other style.
- **Bot label placement and style**: The overlay is positioned absolutely inside the hero container rather than rendered onto the canvas itself. This keeps text rendering in the DOM where it is crisp at all resolutions and accessible, and avoids adding a text-drawing pass to the animation loop.

topics.title: Bot loop-guard escape now times out and returns control to the bot's own algorithm
topics.decisions:
- **Clear visit history on escape exit**: when the escape window closes, the record of recently visited positions is wiped. Without this, the bot would immediately re-detect the same stale loop on the very next tick and start another escape, effectively staying in escape mode indefinitely through a different path. Clearing the history gives the bot a clean slate to resume its own strategy.
- **Trigger only on fresh detection**: the guard now checks whether an escape is already running before starting a new one. The previous code reset the timer unconditionally whenever a loop was detected, which is what caused the timer to hover at one tick below its maximum and never expire. Separating "start an escape" from "continue an escape" makes the two phases independent and the duration predictable.

topics.title: Removed the "Disable Bot" button and its keyboard shortcut from the game screen
topics.decisions: The `botEnabled` flag and the `botEverActive` leaderboard-disqualification flag were deliberately kept intact. The Solo Bot launch path still sets the bot active, human-input suppression still reads the flag, and the leaderboard check still uses it — only the mid-session toggle surface was removed.

topics.title: Removed the ability to switch bots after a game has started
topics.decisions: Bot selection is now exclusively a pre-game action available through the Solo Bot and Bot vs. Bot menu panels. The existing bot status display on the game screen still shows the active bot's name for Solo Bot runs, so players retain visibility without the ability to change mid-game.

topics.title: Arrow keys no longer scroll the page while playing
topics.decisions: The prevention is applied unconditionally in all game states rather than only when a game is active. This avoids the snake moving and the page scrolling simultaneously in any screen, and the placement after the text-field guard ensures the fix never interferes with typing.

entries.recap:
Iron Snake mode now carries the snake's full accumulated length across level advances. When a level goal is reached, the board grows to a size proportional to how long the snake will be by the next goal, and the snake reappears in the same shape it had at the end of the previous level — translated to a new position on the new board, or rotated if the exact placement won't fit. A Warnsdorff-guided layout handles the rare cases where even a rotated fit is impossible, preserving length even when the exact shape cannot be honored. The net effect is that progression feels continuous: the snake the player has grown over several levels carries forward, and the expanding board keeps later levels challenging without becoming impossible.

The snake's head and tail received distinct visual treatments in the same session. The head now rises noticeably above the body segments and displays eyes on the face pointing in the direction of travel, making it immediately clear which end is the front and where the snake is headed. The tail tapers to a narrow point rather than ending in a full-sized cube, giving the snake a clear visual start and finish. Both effects use the same underlying shading logic as the body, so they blend naturally into the isometric scene.

topics.title: Iron Snake levels now preserve snake length and grow the board
topics.decisions:
- **Shape preservation over simple length preservation**: Carrying the snake's exact geometry (not just its length) across a level advance makes the transition feel continuous rather than like a fresh start. Rigid placement tries the identity orientation first so the snake appears in the same shape it was in; rotations are a fallback that still preserve the visual form. A pure length-preserving re-layout was kept as a deeper fallback for cases where the exact shape genuinely cannot fit.
- **40% fill target at goal, not at level start**: Sizing the board so the snake fills 40% of playable cells at the moment the goal is reached means the board feels roomy at the start of each level and tightens naturally as the snake grows toward the goal. A tighter target would make early play cramped; a looser one would make late levels too easy. The floor at the user's chosen grid size ensures level 1 is byte-identical to the old behavior.
- **Warnsdorff heuristic for the layout fallback**: A greedy straight-first walk painted itself into corners on irregular boards with long snakes, causing the fallback to fail and ultimately reset the snake to length 1. Warnsdorff's rule (extend toward the most-constrained neighbor first, break ties by continuing straight) reliably seats long snakes on irregular boards without expensive backtracking, keeping the fallback chain robust at high levels.

topics.title: Snake head now stands taller with direction-facing eyes
topics.decisions: - **Eyes on the leading face only, no fallback**: An earlier version placed eyes on the top face when the leading face was hidden, but this looked unnatural (a snake staring at the sky). The user confirmed it was acceptable to show no eyes when the snake faces away from the camera, keeping the rendering clean and the code simpler.

topics.title: Snake tail now tapers to a point instead of ending in a flat stub
topics.decisions: Extracting `drawPrism` as a shared primitive was chosen over duplicating the face-visibility and shading logic inside `drawSnakeTail`. The isometric shading (which faces are front vs. back, how they are colored) is non-trivial, and duplicating it would have made the two renderers diverge visually over time. Sharing the primitive guarantees the tail shades identically to the body regardless of orientation.

topics.title: Death marker now respects scene depth instead of floating on top
topics.decisions: Using the existing stable painter's sort rather than a separate depth-test pass kept the fix minimal and consistent with how every other object in the scene is ordered. The only exception retained is the off-grid wall-death sliver, which is genuinely outside the board's coordinate space and has no meaningful depth position to sort into.

topics.title: Add project guidance to use Jolli Memory when planning or coding
topics.decisions:
- **Separate branch over bundling with the active feature branch**: The guidance file is unrelated to the progression feature in progress, so mixing it into that branch would add noise to the PR diff and tie a trivial doc change to a longer-running feature. Branching off the upstream default branch instead keeps the diff minimal and lets this land independently in minutes.
- **Project-level guidance file over a memory entry or hook**: A project-level guidance file is loaded into context every session and applies to anyone working in the repo, making it the right home for judgment-based instructions like "when appropriate." A hook would be too rigid for a "when relevant" guideline and would misfire on unrelated tasks.

entries.recap: Iron Snake Mode now gives players a clear visual countdown for the final three fruits of every level. The fruit shifts from its normal red to violet with three remaining, then magenta with two, then near-white on the very last fruit before the level completes. After eating the final fruit and advancing to the next level, the fruit returns to red immediately. The effect applies in both single-player and two-player Iron Snake sessions, where the countdown tracks the combined score of both players against the shared level goal.

topics.title: Iron Snake level finale signaled by three countdown fruit colors
topics.decisions:
- **Three distinct colors rather than a blend or single warning color**: A smooth color interpolation or a single flat "warning" color would not communicate which specific fruit the player is on. Three visually distinct hues — violet, magenta, white — give the player an unambiguous per-fruit countdown, making the level transition feel earned and readable at a glance. The progression toward white on the final fruit was chosen to feel climactic and unmistakable against the dark isometric board.
- **Reusing the existing hunger-tint pattern rather than a new rendering path**: The codebase already computed a color triad before the render switch and passed it into the block-draw call for hunger tinting. Mirroring that exact pattern kept the change minimal and consistent with established conventions, avoiding any new rendering infrastructure or per-object state.
- **Leaving the menu demo screen fruit unchanged**: The animated start-screen background draws decorative fruit with no connection to level state. Touching it would have introduced a dependency where none belongs, and the visual consistency of the menu is unrelated to the in-game countdown cue.

entries.recap:
When a snake dies, the game board now stays visible and frozen rather than immediately switching away. The fatal collision is highlighted on the board in an amber block, and a pulsing banner names exactly how the snake lost — hitting a wall, running into itself, crashing into the opponent, or starving. In two-player mode, the banner describes each snake's fate individually, and head-on collisions get their own message.

The freeze persists until the player presses any key, at which point the normal Game Over screen appears with scores, high-score entry, and leaderboard options all intact. During the freeze, the camera controls remain fully active: players can rotate the board, toggle overhead view, and adjust perspective to inspect the collision from any angle before moving on.

topics.title: Freeze game on death showing loss reason until key pressed
topics.decisions:
- **Deferred callback over rewriting end-game functions**: Rather than splitting or duplicating the end-game logic, the freeze inserts itself as a step in front of the existing functions by storing them as callbacks. This kept all the winner/leaderboard/high-score code untouched and made the change low-risk — the freeze is purely additive, and the finalize step is identical to what ran before.
- **Camera keys fall through during freeze**: The initial implementation dismissed the freeze on any key press. After review, rotate, overhead, and perspective keys were whitelisted to fall through, turning the frozen board into an inspectable "death cam." This required distinguishing between "view" keys and "intent to continue" keys, but the whitelist is small and the UX benefit — being able to spin the board to see exactly how you died — was considered worth the added complexity.
- **DOM overlay banner over canvas text**: The loss reason is shown as a DOM element overlaid on the canvas rather than drawn with canvas text. This mirrors the existing starvation-warning pattern already in the codebase, keeps the canvas rendering code free of text layout concerns, and means the banner inherits CSS animation and accessibility attributes without extra work.

entries.recap:
Iron Snake Mode was added as an optional toggle on the game's main menu, composing with all three existing play configurations (single-player, two-player, and bot-vs-bot). When enabled, each game starts on a freshly generated irregular board shape — a connected region free of narrow one-or-two-cell corridors — rather than the usual square grid. The board outline and grid lines update to trace the true playable area, food only appears on reachable cells, and bots navigate the new shape automatically without any changes to their logic.

Progression works through a level system that replaces the grid-doubling mechanic active in classic mode. Players advance by reaching cumulative score thresholds that grow with each level — 5 points to clear level one, 15 for level two, 30 for level three, and so on — with each level demanding more than the last. On advancing, a brand-new board shape is generated, snakes reset to length one on safe starting positions, and game speed increases slightly. A live countdown in the heads-up display shows exactly how many points remain until the next level, updating with every piece of food eaten.

topics.title: Iron Snake Mode: irregular random board shapes with level goals
topics.decisions:
- **Single collision chokepoint over distributed guards**: Rather than updating bot code, movement loops, and food spawning separately, the mask check was injected only into `inBounds()`, which every collision path already funnels through. This kept the bot API completely unchanged and made the irregular shape an automatic constraint everywhere at once, at the cost of a slightly less transparent call chain.
- **Overlapping rectangles over cellular automata or random walks**: Rectangular rooms with a minimum side of 3 make the no-narrow-corridor property nearly free by construction, overlap guarantees connectivity between rooms, and area is easy to tune. Cellular automata caves and random-walk accretion were considered but rejected as harder to control for minimum corridor width and fill ratio without extensive tuning.
- **Cumulative score goals over per-level resets**: Goals are thresholds on the running total score rather than points earned within a level. This keeps the leaderboard score honest (it is always the true final score), means each goal is crossed exactly once as the snake keeps eating, and avoids a confusing score reset mid-game. The trade-off is that early levels feel shorter since the snake already has momentum.

topics.title: Iron Snake Mode toggle label and placement refined
topics.decisions: - **Rename internals to match the final user-facing name**: Keeping internal identifiers as `arena` while the label read "Iron Snake Mode" would have created a confusing mismatch for future contributors. The full rename (file, exports, DOM ids, CSS classes, state fields, constants) was done in one pass to keep the vocabulary consistent, accepting a slightly larger diff in exchange for a coherent codebase.

topics.title: Level goals made larger with strictly increasing per-level requirements
topics.decisions:
- **Triangular number curve over a flat increment or exponential**: A triangular progression (`5·L(L+1)/2`) gives deltas that grow linearly (5, 10, 15…), which feels fair and predictable to players — each level is harder by a fixed additional amount rather than doubling or jumping unpredictably. A flat increment was rejected as too easy at high levels; an exponential was considered too punishing.
- **Cumulative threshold with a countdown display**: Showing "points remaining to next goal" rather than "points scored this level" lets players track progress against a single moving target without needing to remember what the level started at. The countdown also naturally handles the multiplayer combined-score case with no extra UI.

topics.title: README rewritten to reflect all shipped features accurately
topics.decisions:
- **Codebase exploration before writing**: Rather than editing the README from memory or from the existing text, a subagent was used to read the actual source files first. This avoided the risk of documenting features inaccurately or missing newly added ones, at the cost of a longer round-trip before any edits appeared.
- **"Coming soon" items pruned to only unshipped work**: Isometric 3D and two-player mode were removed from the roadmap and moved into the main feature list, since both are fully implemented. Color blind mode, first-person view, and the "snek" rename were kept as they remain unimplemented. This keeps the roadmap honest and avoids misleading users about what the product currently does.
- **Global leaderboards re-added to roadmap as a distinct item**: The user confirmed that "view other players' high scores" from the deleted README3 should remain in the roadmap, framed as a global/online leaderboard to distinguish it from the local per-mode leaderboards that already exist. Dropping it entirely would have lost a genuine user request captured in the old feedback file.

topics.title: Extra README files removed and content consolidated into one
topics.decisions: Redundant framing from the extra files (separate "future plans" and "user feedback" headers) was dropped rather than preserved as subsections. Folding everything into the existing roadmap keeps the README as a single source of truth and avoids readers needing to check multiple files for the full picture.

entries.recap: The snake game gained an overhead view mode, accessible by pressing the `O` key during any game mode. Activating it collapses the isometric 3D board into a true top-down layout where the board, snake segments, and food all appear as flat squares with no depth or perspective. The view locks out rotation and perspective controls for the duration, and auto-rotation pauses automatically. Pressing `O` a second time returns the board to exactly the isometric angle and perspective settings the player had before switching, preserving any customisation they had made.

topics.title: Overhead top-down view toggle added to snake game board
topics.decisions:
- **Two-pass fix for true flatness**: The first implementation only zeroed the camera angles and perspective, which was not enough — the blocks themselves still had extruded height and appeared as cubes viewed straight-on. Rather than adding a separate rendering path, the block height calculation was made aware of the overhead flag, keeping the fix minimal and contained to a single expression.
- **Save-and-restore over a fixed overhead preset**: When entering overhead view the current isometric settings are saved to private fields and restored on exit, rather than snapping back to hardcoded defaults. This means users who have customised the rotation angle or perspective before pressing `O` get their exact view back, with no loss of state.
- **Input lockout during overhead view**: Rotation and perspective keys are silently ignored while overhead is active, and auto-rotation is suspended. The alternative — allowing those keys to work and immediately re-flattening — would have been confusing. Locking input keeps the overhead view stable and predictable without requiring a separate mode indicator in the UI.

entries.recap:
Bots in Iron Snake mode could previously get locked into permanent approach-and-retreat cycles, approaching a food cell, refusing to enter a tight pocket, retreating, and then repeating the exact same sequence indefinitely. A new loop detection system now tracks each bot's recent movement history and, when it recognises the snake is circling rather than making progress, steers it toward the least-recently-visited safe cell for several ticks to break the cycle. All bot personalities are preserved during normal play — the override only activates when a genuine loop is detected. The same fix was applied to the animated snakes on the title screen.

A complementary starvation system was added to handle the remaining edge case: a bot that wanders productively but can never reach walled-off food. Each snake now has a move budget between meals that scales with the size of the playable board. If a snake exhausts its budget without eating, it dies and the game ends cleanly with a "Starved!" result screen. In two-player modes, the outcome correctly names which snake starved, and simultaneous starvation resolves as a draw. As a snake approaches its limit, its colour gradually shifts from green toward amber and red, and a pulsing warning banner appears over the game board so players know to race for the food.

topics.title: Add starvation timer so stuck bots eventually lose automatically
topics.decisions:
- **Area-based limit over length-based:** The budget scales with how large the playable board is rather than how long the snake is. A bigger board means food can legitimately be farther away, so the limit grows with the board. The user asked whether longer snakes should get more time (a natural assumption), and the decision was to keep the limit board-area-based with a reset-on-eat mechanic — a competent snake never approaches the limit, it only fires as a backstop when food is genuinely unreachable.
- **Reuse existing loss paths rather than a new death type:** Starvation is routed through the same `endGame` / `endGameTwoPlayer` calls already used for collision deaths, with a `deathCause` flag to adjust the displayed text. This kept the win/loss/draw resolution logic untouched and meant two-player starvation draws (both snakes starving on the same tick) were handled for free by the existing symmetry in `endGameTwoPlayer`.
- **Overlay banner over inline HUD text:** The warning is a pulsing pill overlaid on the canvas rather than a line added to the score HUD, so it grabs attention without shifting the game layout. A plain urgent message was chosen over a live countdown to avoid per-tick number flicker that could itself be distracting during play.

topics.title: Add loop guard to break bot oscillation cycles in tight board pockets
topics.decisions:
- **Harness-level guard over per-bot changes:** All 11 bots are stateless pure functions that return only a direction — they have no mechanism to store history or vary their scoring across ticks. Adding memory to each bot would have required changing the shared bot interface and rewriting every bot. A centralized guard at the harness level intercepts the chosen direction only when a loop is actually detected, leaving all bot personalities completely intact during normal play.
- **Confinement ratio added after revisit-count alone proved insufficient:** Initial testing showed that a pure revisit counter (3 hits in a window) cannot catch a ~20-cell "approach, give up, wander back" circuit — on a period-20 loop, no cell recurs 3 times until 60 moves have passed. A secondary confinement signal (distinct cells / total moves below a threshold over a 24-move sample) was added to catch these larger circuits, confirmed by a simulation where the guard broke a bot out of a 20-cell ring to explore 125 cells.

entries.recap: Players can now press T during a game to freeze the board's automatic spinning in place, then press T again to resume it. The pause holds indefinitely until toggled off, giving players full control over their viewing angle without interrupting gameplay. Both the two-player and bot-vs-bot modes display the new shortcut in the on-screen controls bar so players can discover it without consulting documentation.

topics.title: Add keyboard shortcut to pause and resume board auto-rotation
topics.decisions: The pause toggle was implemented as a separate boolean flag rather than reusing the existing timed-override mechanism (which temporarily suppresses rotation after a manual Q/E rotation). Keeping them separate means the T key pause persists indefinitely until the player presses T again, while the timed override continues to work independently for its own purpose. Merging the two would have required tracking intent and complicated the expiry logic.

entries.recap:
The start screen received a substantial visual and interaction overhaul. The most prominent addition is a live animated snake running on an isometric 3D grid, displayed as a hero element at the top of the menu. The snake is driven by the same bot logic used in real gameplay, so it moves intelligently, eats food, and restarts after dying — giving new visitors an immediate sense of the game's style before they press a single button. The canvas fades softly into the background using a CSS mask, and the game title and tagline float over it as an overlay.

The button layout was also redesigned. Bot configuration options — previously shown as always-visible dropdowns — now live inside expandable inline panels that appear only when a player chooses "Solo Bot" or "Bot vs Bot." Each panel contains the relevant bot selectors and a launch button, and collapses cleanly with a cancel action. This keeps the default view focused on the two primary actions (New Game and Two Player) while still making advanced options accessible without a separate screen or modal.

topics.title: Animated isometric snake demo added to the start screen
topics.decisions:
- **Self-contained module over reusing the main game renderer**: The demo was built as an independent class with its own projection, bot helpers, and game loop rather than sharing code with the main `SnakeGame` class. This avoids coupling the menu's lightweight visual to the full game's state machine and lifecycle, making it safe to start and stop independently without risk of interfering with an in-progress game.
- **Canvas edge fade via CSS mask instead of canvas drawing**: The vignette effect that softens the demo canvas into the background is applied as a CSS radial gradient mask rather than being drawn inside the canvas itself. This keeps the rendering code free of presentation concerns and makes the fade trivially adjustable through CSS without touching the animation logic.
- **Bot reuse for the demo snake**: Rather than scripting a fixed movement pattern, the demo runs the same configurable bot used in actual gameplay. This ensures the demo always looks competent and dynamic, and it automatically benefits from any future bot improvements without extra maintenance.

topics.title: Bot and mode selection moved into expandable inline panels
topics.decisions:
- **Expand-in-place panels over a modal or separate screen**: The bot configuration expands inline beneath its trigger button rather than opening a modal overlay or navigating to a new screen. This keeps the interaction lightweight and reversible with a single cancel tap, and avoids the visual disruption of a full overlay for what is a small set of options.
- **Hiding the trigger button while the panel is open**: When a panel opens, its trigger button is hidden rather than left visible in a toggled/active state. This prevents double-tap confusion and keeps the button row uncluttered while the panel is in use, at the cost of a slightly less conventional toggle pattern.

topics.title: Start screen footer added with leaderboards link and GitHub link
topics.decisions: The leaderboards action was demoted to a footer link rather than kept as a full-width button to reflect its lower priority relative to starting a game. Giving it the same visual treatment as the GitHub link (plain text, hover-only color change) keeps the primary call-to-action hierarchy clear without removing the feature from the screen.

entries.recap:
The main menu was redesigned around an animated isometric snake demo that plays automatically when the menu is shown. A rotating 3D board — complete with perspective foreshortening, a checkerboard ground, block shadows, and a bot-driven snake — fills the hero area above the title, giving players an immediate sense of the game's visual style before they start. The animation runs continuously while the menu is visible and pauses cleanly whenever a game or leaderboard screen takes over.

Bot configuration options for the solo-bot and bot-vs-bot modes were moved out of the main menu layout and into expandable inline panels that appear only when the player selects those modes. The menu now shows just three primary actions by default; choosing "Solo Bot" or "Bot vs Bot" reveals a compact panel with a bot picker, description, and dedicated Launch and Cancel buttons. This keeps the menu uncluttered for players who simply want to start a game, while still giving full bot selection to those who want it.

topics.title: Animated rotating snake demo added to the main menu screen
topics.decisions:
- **Self-contained module over reusing the main game renderer**: The demo needed its own isolated game state, bot helpers, and draw loop running independently of any active game session. Sharing the main renderer would have coupled the demo's lifecycle to the game's state machine, making it fragile to pause, restart, or stop cleanly on screen transitions. A standalone class keeps the menu concern fully encapsulated.
- **Continuous camera rotation as the hero visual**: A slowly rotating isometric view communicates the game's 3D aesthetic immediately and creates motion that draws the eye without requiring any user interaction. A static screenshot or a fixed-angle board would have been less distinctive and harder to differentiate from a 2D game at a glance.
- **Radial gradient mask on the canvas edges**: Rather than cropping the canvas with a hard border or a container clip, a CSS mask fades the demo into the dark background. This avoids a visible rectangular frame around the animation and makes the hero feel integrated with the page rather than embedded as a widget.

topics.title: Bot selection moved into expandable inline panels on the menu
topics.decisions:
- **Inline expand panel rather than a modal or separate screen**: Keeping the bot options in-place within the menu flow avoids a context switch and lets the player see their selection alongside the launch button without navigating away. A modal would have required an overlay layer and dismiss logic; a separate screen would have broken the single-page feel of the menu.
- **Hiding the trigger button while the panel is open**: When a panel is open, its trigger button disappears rather than staying visible in a toggled/active state. This prevents the layout from growing awkwardly tall with both the button and the panel stacked, and makes the Cancel button inside the panel the clear way to go back.

topics.title: Footer with leaderboards link and GitHub link added to menu
topics.decisions: Styling the leaderboards button as a plain text link rather than a secondary button visually separates navigational/utility actions from game-start actions. This lets the primary buttons dominate the menu hierarchy without removing the leaderboards feature, and it naturally accommodates the GitHub link sitting beside it at the same weight.

entries.recap: Players can no longer earn a leaderboard entry by briefly enabling the bot during a run and then switching it off before the game ends. The game now tracks whether the bot was active at any point during a run, and any bot involvement — even momentary — permanently disqualifies that run from the name prompt. A new run started without the bot remains fully eligible for the leaderboard as normal.

topics.title: Bot involvement now fully disqualifies a run from the leaderboard name prompt
topics.decisions:
- **Lifetime flag over snapshot check**: Checking only whether the bot was active at the moment the game ended allowed players to game the system by toggling the bot off before dying. Tracking whether the bot was ever active during the run closes this loophole with minimal complexity — a single boolean that is set once and never cleared until the next game starts.
- **Reset on new game, not on toggle-off**: The flag resets only when a new run begins, not when the bot is disabled mid-run. This keeps the disqualification permanent for the lifetime of a single run while still allowing a fresh run to be fully human-played and eligible for the leaderboard.

entries.recap: Scores achieved with bot assistance are now fully excluded from the human leaderboard, even when the bot was only active for part of a run. Previously, a player could turn the bot on mid-game and then switch it off before dying, and the game would still invite them to submit their score. The game now tracks whether the bot was used at any point during a run, and if it was, the name-entry prompt is suppressed entirely at game over. Runs that begin with the bot already enabled are also disqualified from the first moment, with no gap where an early assisted score could be submitted.

topics.title: Bot-assisted runs excluded from leaderboard name prompt
topics.decisions:
- **Lifetime tracking over point-in-time check**: The previous approach only checked whether the bot was active at the instant the game ended, which allowed players to use the bot mid-run and then switch it off before dying to sneak onto the leaderboard. Tracking whether the bot was ever active during the run closes this gap entirely. The trade-off is a small amount of extra state to carry through the run, but the integrity benefit is clear and the cost is negligible.
- **Flag initialized from current bot state at run start**: When a new run begins, the "ever active" flag is seeded from whether the bot is already on, rather than always starting at false. This means a player who starts a run with the bot already enabled is immediately disqualified, with no window where an early-game bot score could slip through.

entries.recap:
The game now supports persistent leaderboards across all three play modes — single player, two-player, and bot vs bot. After finishing a game, players who achieve a top-10 score are shown an inline prompt to enter their name, with their projected rank displayed before they commit. The entry is saved immediately and a confirmation shows the final rank. Players can browse all three leaderboards from the main menu, switching between modes with tabs, and can clear any board individually. Bot-vs-bot results are recorded automatically using each bot's name, keeping that leaderboard populated without any manual input.

Alongside the leaderboard feature, the game-over screen now surfaces two additional stats for every run: the longest snake length reached and the total survival time. In two-player games both players' peak lengths are shown side by side. These same stats feed directly into the leaderboard ranking, so ties on score are broken first by longest snake and then by how long the player survived.

topics.title: Persistent leaderboards with name entry and per-mode rankings
topics.decisions:
- **Qualification check before prompting**: Players are only shown the name-entry form when their result would actually appear in the top 10. Prompting everyone regardless of rank would feel hollow for low scores and train players to dismiss the prompt. The check runs against the current board state before any entry is saved, so the displayed rank estimate is accurate.
- **Bot-vs-bot auto-records without prompting**: Bot games have no human player to name, so both bots' results are written silently using the bot's display name. Prompting for a name in a fully automated game mode would be disruptive and meaningless. This keeps the bot leaderboard populated automatically while reserving the name-entry flow for human players.
- **Last-used name pre-filled in the input**: The name entered most recently is stored separately and pre-populated the next time the prompt appears. This reduces friction for returning players who want to keep the same identity across sessions, while still allowing them to change it each time.

topics.title: Game-over screen shows survival time and longest snake stats
topics.decisions: - **Stats displayed on game-over rather than during play**: Showing running stats mid-game would add visual noise to the play area. Surfacing them only at the end keeps the in-game UI clean while still giving players a meaningful post-run summary to reflect on.

topics.title: Keyboard input blocked from triggering game actions while typing a name
topics.decisions: Intercepting at the top of the key handler rather than disabling individual bindings keeps the fix in one place and covers all current and future shortcuts uniformly. Disabling specific keys per-field would require updating multiple code paths whenever new shortcuts are added.

entries.recap:
The game now tracks and persists scores across sessions through a full leaderboard system. Three separate rankings — one each for single-player, two-player, and bot-versus-bot — are accessible from the main menu and from the game-over screen. Each leaderboard holds the top ten entries, sorted by score and broken ties by longest snake length and survival time.

At the end of every game, players see a stat summary showing their longest snake and how long they survived. When a score is good enough to crack the top ten, a name-entry prompt appears so the player can claim their rank; the game remembers the last name used to make repeat entries faster. Bot-versus-bot results are recorded automatically under each bot's name with no prompt needed. After saving, a confirmation line tells the player exactly where they landed on the board.

topics.title: Per-mode leaderboards with name entry and stat tracking
topics.decisions:
- **Separate leaderboards per mode rather than one unified table**: Mixing single-player scores with two-player or bot scores would make the rankings meaningless — a bot-versus-bot run produces scores under completely different conditions than a solo game. Keeping three independent boards lets each mode's rankings reflect comparable play.
- **Qualify-before-prompting rather than always asking for a name**: Interrupting every player with a name prompt regardless of their score would feel annoying and devalue the leaderboard. Checking eligibility first means only genuinely competitive runs trigger the prompt, which keeps the interaction feel rewarding rather than routine.
- **Auto-record bot-versus-bot results with no prompt**: Bot runs are deterministic and unattended — there is no human present to type a name. Recording both bots automatically (using their display names) keeps the bot leaderboard populated without requiring any interaction, while still letting players browse those results for comparison.

entries.recap:
The Snake game gained two new competitive modes: a local two-player mode where one player uses WASD and the other uses arrow keys, and a fully automated bot-versus-bot mode where two independently configured bots compete on the same board. Both modes start immediately on launch rather than waiting for a keypress, and the game loop resolves both snakes' moves at the same instant each tick, treating simultaneous head-on collisions as a draw rather than giving either player an advantage.

The menu, in-game display, and game-over screen were all updated to reflect the active mode. Players can now select a separate bot profile for each snake before starting a bot match. During play, per-player scores appear color-coded to match each snake, and the bot controls are hidden entirely in two-player modes. After a match ends, the game-over screen announces the winner and shows both players' final scores, with a menu button and keyboard shortcut available to return to the main screen at any time.

topics.title: Two-player and bot-versus-bot game modes added to Snake
topics.decisions:
- **Separate key bindings for two-player mode**: In two-player mode, Player 1 is locked to WASD and Player 2 to arrow keys, rather than letting both players share all keys. Sharing keys would cause one player's input to accidentally move the other's snake, making simultaneous play impossible. Single-player mode retains both key sets for convenience.
- **Simultaneous collision resolution**: Both snakes' new head positions are computed before either move is applied, and a head-to-head collision is treated as a draw rather than giving priority to whichever snake was processed first. Processing them sequentially would introduce an unfair advantage for whichever player happened to be evaluated first in the code.
- **Opponent snake passed into bot helpers**: Rather than giving bots a global view of the board, the opponent's body is injected only into the path-finding helpers (`simulateMove`, `analyzePosition`) used during that bot's turn. This keeps the existing bot interface stable and avoids rewriting every bot implementation while still letting bots make collision-aware decisions against a second snake.

topics.title: Menu and in-game UI updated to support multiple game modes
topics.decisions:
- **Hide bot controls entirely in two-player modes**: Rather than disabling the bot toggle button, it and the bot profile selector are hidden when a two-player mode is active. Leaving them visible but disabled would invite confusion about whether the bot was influencing the game; hiding them makes the control surface match the actual game rules.
- **Color-coded player scores throughout**: Player 1 scores are styled green (matching the snake color) and Player 2 scores blue throughout the game and game-over screens. Consistent color association reduces the cognitive load of tracking which number belongs to which snake, especially during fast play.

entries.recap:
The snake game gained two new multiplayer modes in this commit. Two local players can now compete on the same keyboard, with Player 1 using WASD and Player 2 using the arrow keys. Both snakes move simultaneously each game tick, collisions between them are detected fairly, and a head-to-head crash results in a draw. The game-over screen now shows both players' scores and declares a winner.

A bot-vs-bot spectator mode was also introduced. Players can select a different AI profile for each side from the main menu, then watch the two bots compete on the shared grid. Each bot runs its own decision logic every tick and is aware of the opponent's body as an obstacle, making the competition meaningful across different bot strategies. The auto-rotating isometric view activates automatically in this mode, giving it a polished hands-off presentation.

topics.title: Two-player local multiplayer mode added to snake game
topics.decisions:
- **Separate key sets per player**: Player 1 is locked to WASD and Player 2 to arrow keys in two-player mode, rather than sharing both input sets as in single-player. This prevents one player's input from accidentally controlling the other's snake and makes the control split unambiguous on a shared keyboard.
- **Simultaneous collision resolution**: Both snakes move and are checked for collisions in the same game tick, including a head-to-head check, rather than resolving one player's move before the other's. This ensures neither player has an unfair advantage from move ordering, and a simultaneous crash into each other correctly results in a draw.
- **Shared food and grid**: Both snakes compete for the same single food item on a shared grid rather than each having their own. This keeps the competitive dynamic tight and reuses the existing food-spawning and grid-expansion logic with only minor adjustments to account for combined snake length.

topics.title: Bot-vs-bot spectator mode added to snake game
topics.decisions:
- **Independent bot instances with shared awareness**: Each bot runs its own decision function and receives the opponent's body as blocked space, rather than using a single shared planner. This keeps each bot's strategy self-contained and makes it straightforward to mix different bot profiles against each other.
- **Opt-in opponent awareness via optional field**: The opponent snake is passed through an optional field on the bot state type rather than making it required. This preserves full backward compatibility with existing single-player bot implementations that don't need to know about an opponent.

entries.recap: A new bot called Sweeper was added to the game's roster of AI opponents. It navigates the grid in a back-and-forth lawnmower pattern, sweeping each row horizontally and stepping down to the next row at the end, aiming for full grid coverage rather than purely chasing food or reacting to other snakes. The bot balances this pattern against survival: it continuously checks whether it can still reach its own tail and how much open space remains, and will abandon the sweep path when following it would put it in danger. Players can now select Sweeper alongside the existing ten bots.

topics.title: New sweeper bot added with lawnmower grid coverage pattern
topics.decisions:
- **Scoring over hard rules**: The bot uses a weighted numeric score for each candidate direction rather than a strict rule chain. This keeps safety constraints (reaching the tail, maintaining open space) dominant while still nudging the bot toward the sweep pattern — the pattern preference adds only a modest bonus, so the bot will deviate from the lawnmower path when doing so would trap it.
- **Top-to-bottom, not arbitrary sweep axis**: Rows are swept horizontally and the bot advances downward, rather than sweeping columns or alternating axes. This matches the most natural reading order for a grid and keeps the directional logic simple — a single row-parity check determines the preferred horizontal direction, and an "at row end" check triggers the downward turn.
- **Mild penalty for moving upward**: Rather than forbidding upward movement entirely, the bot applies a small score penalty for it. This preserves escape options when the snake is cornered while still discouraging backtracking against the intended sweep direction.

entries.recap:
A new Sweeper bot joined the roster of available snake strategies. It navigates the grid in a lawnmower pattern, sweeping each row in alternating directions and stepping downward at the end of each pass. Unlike purely reactive bots, the Sweeper treats coverage as its primary goal, with food-seeking and open-space awareness playing a supporting role.

The bot stays alive by treating the ability to reach its own tail as a near-disqualifying condition for any move, so it will break from the sweep pattern when necessary to avoid trapping itself. Players selecting the Sweeper can expect methodical, grid-wide coverage under normal conditions, with graceful deviation when the pattern would otherwise be fatal.

topics.title: New Sweeper bot covers the grid in a lawnmower pattern
topics.decisions:
- **Safety as a hard gate, sweep preference as a soft nudge**: The bot treats the ability to reach its own tail as a near-disqualifying condition rather than just another scoring factor. This keeps the snake alive even when the ideal sweep direction would trap it, accepting occasional deviation from the lawnmower path in exchange for survival. A purely pattern-driven bot would be elegant but fragile.
- **Scoring over deterministic rules**: Rather than hard-coding turn logic, every candidate direction is scored and the highest wins. This lets food proximity and open space naturally override the sweep preference when the situation demands it, without requiring explicit exception handling for every edge case. The trade-off is that the sweep pattern is a tendency rather than a guarantee.
- **Top-to-bottom sweep direction**: Moving downward through rows was chosen as the primary axis of progression, with upward movement penalized. This gives the bot a consistent, predictable coverage direction and avoids oscillating between rows, at the cost of making it slightly less adaptive when food appears above the current row.

entries.recap: A new snake bot called Zigzag joined the roster of available opponents. It moves in a sawtooth weaving pattern, alternating left and right turns as it advances toward food, giving it a visually distinct style compared to the existing bots. The bot also applies a wall-avoidance penalty near the edges of the grid, where weaving movement is most likely to cause self-entrapment, and falls back gracefully when all available moves are risky rather than failing outright.

topics.title: New zigzag snake bot that weaves toward food
topics.decisions:
- **Safety-first scoring with a hard floor**: Moves that leave the snake unable to reach its own tail are scored at a large negative value rather than being filtered out entirely. This preserves a fallback path when all moves are dangerous, letting the bot pick the least-bad option rather than returning null and crashing.
- **Perpendicular-turn bias over strict path-following**: Rather than building a dedicated zigzag planner, the bot adds a flat score bonus whenever the chosen direction is perpendicular to the current heading. This keeps the implementation simple and composable with the food-pursuit and safety logic, at the cost of the weave being opportunistic rather than geometrically precise.
- **Wall-margin penalty to protect the weave near edges**: Zigzagging close to a wall tends to trap the snake faster than it would a straight-moving bot, so a score penalty is applied when the next head position is within one cell of any border. This trades a small amount of aggressive food pursuit near edges for meaningfully better survival near boundaries.

entries.recap: A new Zigzag bot joined the roster of available snake bots. Unlike the existing bots that move in spirals, along edges, or reactively toward food, the Zigzag bot weaves back and forth in a sawtooth pattern as it advances — turning perpendicular to its current heading whenever it safely can. The bot still pursues food and avoids trapping itself, but its movement has a visually distinctive alternating rhythm. Players can now select Zigzag alongside the nine previously available bots.

topics.title: New Zigzag bot that weaves in sawtooth patterns toward food
topics.decisions:
- **Perpendicular-turn bias over straight-line movement**: The bot awards a score bonus for turning perpendicular to the current heading rather than continuing straight. This is a lightweight way to encode the sawtooth weaving behavior without a separate state machine or turn counter — the scoring function naturally alternates directions as long as perpendicular moves are safe and competitive.
- **Safety gate before style**: Tail reachability is checked first and, if it fails, the direction is scored far below any safe alternative regardless of food proximity or weaving preference. This ensures the aesthetic zigzag pattern never overrides survival, accepting that the bot may temporarily move straight when weaving would be dangerous.
- **Wall-proximity penalty**: An additional penalty is applied when the next head position is within one cell of any edge. Zigzagging near walls is disproportionately risky because perpendicular turns quickly run out of space, so the penalty discourages the bot from entering those corridors in the first place.

entries.recap: A new bot called Spiral joined the snake game's roster of playable opponents. Rather than chasing food directly or patrolling edges, it moves in a clockwise sweeping pattern across the grid, running straight until it nears a wall and then turning inward to continue the spiral. Food collection is still part of its behavior, but the spiral motion takes priority, giving it a visually distinct movement style compared to the existing bots. The bot also checks at every step that it can still reach its own tail, keeping it from trapping itself as the spiral tightens.

topics.title: New spiral movement bot added to snake game
topics.decisions:
- **Straight-first, clockwise-second priority**: The bot awards a higher score to continuing straight than to turning clockwise, with counter-clockwise receiving nothing. This produces a natural spiral: the snake runs along a straight path until a wall forces a clockwise turn, then repeats. A pure clockwise-always rule would cause tight circles; the straight preference creates wider, grid-sweeping loops.
- **Tail-reachability as a hard gate**: Any move that leaves the snake unable to reach its own tail is scored at a large negative value, with only a small partial credit for reachable open area. This keeps the spiral from painting itself into a corner, trading some spiral purity for survival safety.
- **Moderate food pursuit layered on top**: Food proximity and path-to-food scores are included but weighted lower than the spiral and safety signals. This means the bot will detour toward food when a clear path exists, but will not abandon the spiral pattern to chase food aggressively — a deliberate balance between score-seeking and pattern consistency.

entries.recap: Three new bot opponents were added to the snake game, each with a distinct play style. The Ambusher stakes out the center of the board and only chases food when it spawns nearby, making it a patient, positional opponent. The Chaser relentlessly pursues the shortest route to food and switches to a defensive tail-following mode when the direct path becomes dangerous. The Nomad actively steers away from its own body trail, spreading across fresh territory and picking up food along the way rather than hunting it directly. All three bots are now selectable alongside the existing roster.

topics.title: Three new snake bot personalities added to the game
topics.decisions:
- **Distinct behavioral identities over parameter tuning**: Each bot was given a fundamentally different scoring philosophy rather than being a re-tuned version of an existing bot. The Ambusher anchors to a spatial position, the Chaser maximizes food-path efficiency, and the Nomad penalizes body proximity. This makes each bot feel meaningfully different to play against and avoids a situation where bots are indistinguishable in practice.
- **Shared survival floor across all bots**: All three bots use the same tail-reachability check as a hard constraint — moves that would leave the snake unable to reach its own tail are scored far below any safe move. This keeps the new bots from dying trivially while still allowing their distinct personalities to express themselves in safe situations.
- **Body-proximity penalty as the Nomad's core mechanic**: Rather than rewarding exploration abstractly, the Nomad directly counts nearby body segments and applies a steep per-segment penalty. This produces emergent trail-avoidance behavior without requiring explicit path history tracking, keeping the implementation simple while achieving the intended wandering effect.

entries.recap: A new Spiral bot was added to the game's roster of playable bots. Unlike the existing bots that react primarily to food or threats, the Spiral bot attempts to sweep the grid in wide clockwise arcs, turning inward whenever it approaches a wall. Players can now select it alongside the existing options and observe a distinctly different movement personality — one that prioritizes grid coverage over direct food pursuit. The bot still avoids collisions and self-trapping, so it plays safely while maintaining its spiral character.

topics.title: New spiral bot that sweeps the grid in clockwise patterns
topics.decisions:
- **Scoring bias over hard-coded turn rules**: Rather than forcing a fixed turn sequence, the bot uses a continuous scoring system that nudges movement clockwise while still allowing the safety layer to override when the snake is in danger. This keeps the spiral behavior emergent and graceful under pressure — a rigid turn rule would cause the snake to walk into walls or its own body when the ideal clockwise move is blocked.
- **Straight preferred over clockwise turn by default**: Straight movement scores slightly higher than a clockwise turn (1200 vs 800) so the snake extends each leg of the spiral as far as possible before turning. Turning too eagerly would produce tight, inefficient loops rather than wide sweeping spirals that cover more of the grid.
- **Moderate food weighting**: Food pursuit is included but kept at a lower priority than spiral geometry. A bot that aggressively chases food would abandon the spiral pattern constantly, making it indistinguishable from the explorer or hunter bots. The modest food weight means the snake will still eat opportunistically without breaking its sweep pattern.

entries.recap: A new playable snake bot called Nomad was added to the game. Unlike the existing bots, the Nomad actively steers away from its own body trail, favoring open, unexplored areas of the board. It still picks up food along the way, but exploration takes priority over aggressive food-chasing. The Nomad is now available for selection alongside the seven previously existing bots.

topics.title: New Nomad bot added that avoids its own trail to explore open space
topics.decisions:
- **Trail-avoidance as the defining behavior**: The bot's personality is built around penalizing proximity to its own body segments rather than, say, maximizing territory control or aggressive food pursuit. Closer segments carry a heavier penalty, creating a gradient that nudges the snake outward into unexplored space. This produces emergent wandering behavior without requiring explicit path planning to "new" areas.
- **Food pursuit kept moderate**: Unlike the hunter bot, the nomad scores food paths positively but with lower weight, and applies a smaller distance-to-food penalty. This keeps the bot functional (it won't ignore food) while ensuring the trail-avoidance and open-space rewards dominate its movement decisions in normal play.
- **Safety floor preserved**: Moves that break the tail-reachability constraint are scored at a large negative value (with a small reachable-area offset), ensuring the nomad still avoids self-trapping even while wandering. This reuses the existing position-analysis helpers rather than implementing a separate safety check.

entries.recap: Two new bot personalities joined the snake game's roster. The Ambusher stakes out the center of the board and holds position, only breaking into a chase when food spawns close enough to strike — giving it a patient, territorial feel that looks noticeably different from existing bots. The Chaser takes the opposite approach, always hunting the shortest route to food and only pulling back when the direct path would leave it cornered, at which point it shadows its own tail to stay alive until a safe opening appears. Both bots are now available for players to select alongside the existing lineup.

topics.title: Two new snake bot personalities added: Ambusher and Chaser
topics.decisions:
- **Distinct behavioral archetypes over parameter tuning**: Rather than creating bots that differ only in how aggressively they weight food distance, each bot was given a fundamentally different spatial strategy — one anchored to the center, one always chasing. This produces more visibly different play styles that players can observe and distinguish, at the cost of more code to maintain per bot.
- **Survival as a hard constraint, not a scored preference**: Both bots treat tail-reachability as a near-disqualifying condition (a large negative score floor) rather than just another factor in the scoring sum. This ensures the bots rarely self-trap even when food is very close, trading some short-term food efficiency for long-term survival.
- **Strike threshold for the Ambusher**: The ambusher's switch from passive to aggressive mode uses a dynamic threshold tied to grid size rather than a fixed tile count. This keeps the behavior proportional across different board sizes without requiring separate tuning per grid configuration.

entries.recap: A new Nomad bot is now available in the snake game alongside the existing roster of bot personalities. Where other bots focus on hunting food or hugging edges, the Nomad actively steers away from its own body trail, preferring open, unvisited areas of the board. It still collects food along the way, but exploration takes priority over efficiency. Players can now select the Nomad as their bot of choice and observe a noticeably different movement style — one that wanders and spreads across the board rather than coiling or charging.

topics.title: New Nomad bot that steers away from its own trail to explore open space
topics.decisions:
- **Trail avoidance as the core mechanic**: The bot's defining behavior is a strong penalty for moving near its own body, rather than simply chasing food or maximizing open space. This creates a wandering, exploratory character that feels meaningfully different from existing bots. The trade-off is that the snake may take suboptimal paths to food, but that inefficiency is intentional — it produces the nomadic personality.
- **Moderate food pursuit rather than aggressive or passive**: Food scoring is included but weighted lower than in a hunter-style bot. Completely ignoring food would make the bot uncompetitive; over-weighting it would collapse the nomad behavior into something indistinguishable from existing bots. The chosen balance keeps the bot viable while preserving its distinct identity.
- **Tail-reachability as a safety floor**: Moves that leave the snake unable to reach its own tail are heavily penalized rather than outright forbidden, with a partial credit for reachable area. This avoids the bot painting itself into a corner while still allowing it to take calculated risks in open space — a stricter hard cutoff would conflict with the exploratory playstyle.

entries.recap: A new bot called Ambusher joined the snake game's roster of playable opponents. Unlike bots that chase food across the entire board, the Ambusher holds a position near the center of the grid and only commits to an aggressive pursuit when food spawns within a short distance. The strike range adjusts automatically to the size of the board, keeping the bot's behavior consistent across different grid configurations. Survival takes absolute priority: the bot will always choose a safe path over a scoring opportunity if the alternative risks trapping itself.

topics.title: New ambusher bot that lurks centrally and strikes when food is nearby
topics.decisions:
- **Central ambush point over food-chasing**: The bot was designed to treat the grid center as its default anchor rather than always pursuing food. This keeps the snake in a position where food spawning anywhere on the board is roughly equidistant, reducing wasted movement and keeping the snake compact. The trade-off is that the bot may score more slowly when food consistently spawns far from center, but it avoids the erratic long-distance chases that expose other bots to self-collision.
- **Dynamic strike threshold over a fixed distance**: The strike range scales with grid size (30% of the grid, floored at 3) rather than using a hard-coded tile count. This keeps the bot's aggression proportional across different board sizes, so it behaves consistently whether the grid is small or large. A fixed threshold would make the bot either too passive on large grids or recklessly aggressive on small ones.
- **Tail reachability as a hard survival gate**: Any move that leaves the snake unable to reach its own tail is scored at a near-floor value regardless of food proximity or positional advantage. This prioritizes long-term survival over short-term scoring opportunities, accepting that the bot will occasionally pass up food to avoid trapping itself.
